title: Conflict detection is structurally unreachable at both call sites

findingDetails.evidence: payload and requestFingerprint are the same idempotencySemantics object (memory-save 3570-3571; memory-crud-update 239-253 structurally identical), so payloadHash always equals requestFingerprintHash; receiptKey is derived from requestFingerprintHash (idempotency-receipts.ts:88-97), so any key match implies a payload match and the conflict branch (idempotency-receipts.ts:136-137) plus markResponseWithReceiptStoreConflict can never fire.
findingDetails.why: The documented 'idempotency key conflict' protection and the force-in-key justification at memory-save.ts:3562-3565 describe an event that cannot occur (a force-flip yields a key miss, not a conflict). It fails safe, but advertises protection it does not provide and leaves unreachable code that operators may trust when enabling the flag.
findingDetails.recommendation: Either feed the FULL request payload (not the fingerprint subset) into the payload hash so same-key/different-payload conflicts can actually be detected, or remove the conflict/markResponseWithReceiptStoreConflict machinery and correct the comments to state that distinct logical requests always produce distinct keys.

title: Replay can return a deleted memory id via the planner-save bypass

findingDetails.evidence: The replay active-row query selects/compares content_hash only and never compares the live row id to the id in the cached response_payload. Canonical/planner saves skip the entire idempotency block (planner branch returns at 3636-3644 before the store at 3756), so a hard-delete (FK nulls receipt.memory_id at vector-index-schema.ts:668 while response_payload keeps the old id) followed by a planner re-create of identical content leaves receipt_A unrefreshed; a later direct save then matches content_hash and replays a response reporting the deleted id.
findingDetails.why: A caller receives a success response whose data.id points at a memory that no longer exists; a follow-up memory_update by that id fails. The active-row recheck was added precisely to prevent stale replays but only guards content, not identity, and identity can diverge through the planner path.
findingDetails.recommendation: Select id in the active-row query and honor the replay only when it equals extractMemoryIdFromResponse(lookup.response) (and/or the receipt's stored memory_id); otherwise drop the receipt and fall through. Apply the same id check in memory-crud-update.ts:262-267.

title: Receipt TTL is enforced only at daemon boot

findingDetails.evidence: pruneExpiredIdempotencyReceipts has a single caller, executed once during init; no periodic or per-write sweep exists (idempotency-receipts.ts:258-273 is otherwise uncalled in non-test code).
findingDetails.why: These daemons are long-lived/warm; a daemon running longer than the 30-day TTL never sweeps, so receipts grow unbounded between restarts and rows past TTL persist. Staleness is mitigated by the active-row recheck, so impact is bounded growth plus a TTL guarantee weaker than the comment implies — relevant before turning the flag on for a persistent daemon.
findingDetails.recommendation: Add a bounded periodic sweep (e.g. opportunistically on write or on a timer) in addition to the boot prune, or document that TTL only applies across restarts.

title: Prune query cannot use the receipts index

findingDetails.evidence: The only index is on (operation, created_at DESC) (vector-index-schema.ts:630-631) but prune filters WHERE updated_at < datetime('now', ?); no code path ever issues an UPDATE (storeIdempotencyReceipt uses ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING), so updated_at is immutable and equals created_at.
findingDetails.why: The sweep does a full table scan for no behavioral gain; pruning on the indexed created_at would be identical and index-backed.
findingDetails.recommendation: Prune on created_at (indexed), or add an index on updated_at if a future DO UPDATE path is intended.
